text.title: CQRS Antipatterns
text.summary: Two critical CQRS violations: commands returning full read models, and queries with state-modifying side effects.

antipattern.description: Command handler returns a DTO or full entity after saving, forcing the handler to also build a read projection inline.
antipattern.reason: Couples write and read optimization. Breaks the single-responsibility of commands. Prevents independent scaling of read and write sides.

example.code: class CreateOrderHandler: async def handle(self, command) -> OrderDto: # Wrong return type order = Order.create(...) await self._repo.save(order) return OrderDto.from_entity(order) # Build read model in command handler

class CreateOrderHandler: async def handle(self, command: PlaceOrderCommand) -> UUID: order = Order.create(id=command.order_id, ...) await self._repo.save(order) return order.id # Or return None; caller uses a separate query to fetch state

antipattern.description: Query handler modifies state (increments view count, updates last-seen timestamp) as part of returning data.
antipattern.reason: Callers assume reads are idempotent and safe to retry. Hidden writes violate this assumption, cause double-counting, and break eventual consistency reasoning.

example.code: class GetOrderHandler: async def handle(self, query): order = await self._read_store.get(query.order_id) order["view_count"] += 1 # Side effect in a query! await self._read_store.set(order) return order
